figures.caption: Figure 1: Distribution of entities by claims in the Wikidata category Artist. A small number of entities are associated with many claims, while the majority (longtail) are associated with few relations. The red dashed line indicates the Pareto cut-off, while the green dashed line marks the head–tail threshold

Table 2: Results on TailNLG long-tail vs head entitities verbalizations by data type and language. Metrics include BERTScore Rescaled (BERTr), BLEU, chrF++ (chrF), ROUGE1 (R-1) , ROUGE2 (R2), ROUGEL (R-L) and Perplexity (PPL). Asterisks (∗) denote a statistically significant difference (p < 0.05).

Figure 2: PPL distributions for head and long-tail entities across models (log10 scale). Boxes represent the interquartile range (IQR, Q1–Q3) with the median shown as a line inside the box. Statistical significance was assessed using the Mann-Whitney U test: *** p < 0.001, ns = not significant.
Table 11: Performance comparison of models on long-tail triples from TailNLG and WebNLG test set. Asterisks (∗) denote a statistically significant difference with p < 0.05.
Table 5: Distribution of gold and silver verbalizations by language in TailNLG.

Table 10: Performance comparison of models broken down by entity type (long-tail / top-head) and annotation quality (silver / gold). Asterisks (∗) denote a statistically significant difference between silver and gold within the same type (p < 0.05, Mann-Whitney U test).
Table 4: Distribution of data entries across categories and entity types.
Figure 3: Examples of Chain, sibling and mixed triples configuration
Table 7: Comparison of models by type. Asterisks (∗) denote a statistically significant difference with p < 0.05.
Table 8: Average scores for different languages for each model (zero-shot). Asterisks (∗) denote a statistically significant difference with p < 0.05.
Table 9: Average scores for different quality levels (silver, gold) for each model (zero-shot). Asterisks (∗) denote a statistically significant difference with p < 0.05.
